title: Clinical Case Study: Misdiagnosed Major Depressive Disorder in Bipolar II Presentation
content:
Patient Background: 28-year-old female presenting with severe fatigue, anhedonia, and feelings of worthlessness persisting for 3 months.
Initial Diagnosis: Major Depressive Disorder (MDD). Prescribed an SSRI (Sertraline).
Clinical Course: Two weeks into SSRI treatment, the patient experienced a sudden, dramatic shift in mood. She reported needing only 2 hours of sleep per night, feeling "electrified," and started three new business ventures, accompanied by rapid, pressured speech.
Re-evaluation: The SSRI induced a hypomanic episode, revealing an underlying Bipolar II Disorder that was previously masked by the predominant depressive symptoms.
Successful Therapeutic Pathway: The SSRI was immediately discontinued. The patient was transitioned to a mood stabilizer (Lamotrigine) combined with Quetiapine for acute hypomanic symptom control. 
Long-term Outcome: Patient stabilized within 3 weeks. Diagnosis permanently revised to Bipolar II Disorder. This case underscores the high risk of antidepressant-induced mania in patients with complex, layered depressive presentations lacking thorough screening for past hypomanic episodes.
